question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
The purpose of this study is to systematically review
clinical studies of VTE prophylaxis in stroke
patients. The emphasis is in acute CI but we also
analysed studies addressing the rehabilitation phase of
the disease and patients with acute cerebral haemorrhage
(CH) or subarachnoid haemorrhage. An effort 
is made to distinguish the effects of the interventions
on different outcomes such as low-risk calf vein
thrombosis and higher-risk events such as proximal
DVT and PE
Methods
We performed a systematic review of the literature on
thromboprophylaxis in stroke. Articles were included
only if they evaluated one or more active treatments
versus placebo, no intervention or other treatment
modalities in adult (minimum 18 years) stroke patients.
Clinical trials and systematic reviews were searched for

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers: nan

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers:
Clinical trials and systematic reviews were searched for in
MEDLINE, COCHRANE, LILACS and SciELO databases.
The search strategy was developed for MEDLINE
(available from the authors upon request) and
adapted for the other databases. Studies presented in
Neurological and Cerebrovascular Diseases Congresses
and only published in abstract form from 1998 to August
2004 were also searched for in the following journals:
Stroke, Cerebrovascular Diseases, Circulation, Journal of
Neurology, Neurology, Arquivos de Neuro-psiquiatria.

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers:
The reference lists of all included articles and other pertinent
published articles and reviews were also scrutinized
in search for additional clinical studies of interest.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers: nan

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers: nan

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers:
Two authors (CA and GRF) independently reviewed
all titles and abstracts of the initially found studies for
further assessment. Abstracts selected by any one of the
authors were by necessity thoroughly analysed in the
complete publication.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers:
Two authors (CA and GRF) independently reviewed
all titles and abstracts of the initially found studies for
further assessment. Abstracts selected by any one of the
authors were by necessity thoroughly analysed in the
complete publication.

question: Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
answers:
Twenty-five studies evaluated
UFH or LMWH/heparinoids against each other, placebo
or aspirin in the acute phase of CI. Twelve studies
evaluated mechanical devices or other antithrombotics
(aspirin, dextran or Warfarin) in the acute or rehabilitation
phase of stroke or low-dose anticoagulation in
CH.

Table 1 Trials and reviews: prevention of venous thromboembolism prophylaxis after stroke

question: Were all relevant study results collected for use in the synthesis?
answers: Rank 1 Clear: Has a clear quote that makes the jugment easy to make

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers:
Articles were rated according to perceived scientific
value following Cochrane Criteria  as follows:
A - Low risk of bias: Large (100 patients or more)
randomized controlled clinical trials including
systematic DVT evaluation in acute stroke patients
and with all quality criteria met, i.e.
appropriate randomization and blinding processes
clearly described and <20% loss to follow-up;
also, systematic reviews that included at least one
trial with these characteristics.
B - Moderate risk of bias: Clinical trials of lower scientific
value including small number (<100) of
patients or many losses for follow-up.
C - High risk of bias: Studies with no systematic search
for DVT, using historical controls, non-randomized,
not presenting results by groups or with other
major problems

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
answers: nan

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers:
Four studies - two large studies without
prospective evaluation [4,5], one with insufficient data
reported  and one observational  - suggest a
modest beneficial effect on DVT and PE rates, with very
large numbers of patients needed to treat in order to
prevent a single episode. The observational study suggested
that aspirin plus IPC is not effective . Aspirin
also seemed ineffective in a large retrospective study of
patients undergoing rehabilitation for stroke .
We performed a pooled analysis of data from two
large prospective trials evaluating the effects of aspirin
in patients with acute CI

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers: nan

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
We performed a pooled analysis of data from two
large prospective trials evaluating the effects of aspirin
in patients with acute CI (Fig. 1) [4,5]. No prospective
evaluation for VTE was made in these studies. PE
events were clinically diagnosed, with no formal predefinition
of diagnostic criteria; and physicians were 
aware of the allocation of patients either to aspirin or
no treatment.

The incidence of
clinically diagnosed pulmonary embolism was compared in patients
receiving aspirin (160 to 300 mg/day) early in the clinical
course (up to 48 h) or not receiving it.

Heterogeneity
chi-squared  1/4 0.27 (d.f.  1/4 1) P  1/4 0.602

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
Table 1 Trials and reviews: prevention of venous thromboembolism prophylaxis after stroke

We performed a pooled analysis of data from two
large prospective trials evaluating the effects of aspirin
in patients with acute CI (Fig. 1) [4,5]. No prospective
evaluation for VTE was made in these studies. PE
events were clinically diagnosed, with no formal predefinition
of diagnostic criteria; and physicians were
aware of the allocation of patients either to aspirin or
no treatment. Despite these limitations and the relatively
low number of reported events, the results of the
pooled analysis strongly suggest a mild protective effect
of aspirin against VTE or at least PE, its most severe
manifestation

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
Some particulars of the present study should be
acknowledged. No unpublished study was included.
Pooled analysis of data was only made for the use of
aspirin. We believe meta-analyses should only be performed
when there is adequate comparability of the
trials  and this was not the case with many questions
here addressed. We did not exclude studies with no
prospective evaluation of VTE episodes and preferred
instead to classify the studies as to their general scientific
validity. Lastly, we did not evaluate potential differences
between diverse formulations of LMWH or
between them and heparinoids.
